items.myth: Inference is just a smaller version of training.
items.reality: Inference is forward-only with fixed θ — no loss, no labels, no backward pass, no optimizer. Engineering goals (latency, throughput) differ entirely from training.
items.example: A ResNet training job uses backward() and GPU hours; the same ResNet at serving runs one forward pass per image in under 30 ms.

items.myth: Once trained, the model never needs labels again.
items.reality: Inference on new x needs no labels, but monitoring, drift detection, and periodic retraining still require labeled evaluation data and refreshed training sets.
items.example: Live fraud scoring is label-free per transaction, but weekly dashboards compare predictions to confirmed fraud labels from investigations.

items.myth: predict() and fit() can be used interchangeably.
items.reality: fit(X, y) learns parameters and requires labels; predict(X) applies learned parameters and must not be called with y as a training substitute on production data.
items.example: clf.fit(X_new, y_guess) in a webhook corrupts θ; clf.predict(X_new) is the correct inference call.

items.myth: Training and inference always run on the same hardware.
items.reality: Training often uses multi-GPU clusters; inference may run on CPUs, edge devices, or specialized inference chips optimized for batch forward passes.
items.example: LLM fine-tuning on 8 GPUs; chat inference served from a distilled model on 2 CPU replicas with batching.
